title: Data Curation
lesson:
Data curation is the craft of choosing what goes into your training set, and what stays out. Curation is not the same as cleaning. Cleaning fixes broken examples (bad formatting, junk characters). Curation decides which examples deserve to be there at all. Think of it as the difference between scrubbing the vegetables versus deciding which vegetables to put in the pot.

Here is the rule that surprises most beginners: when fine-tuning a model, quality beats quantity, hard. A model learns by imitating the examples you feed it. If 5% of your examples are sloppy, rude, or wrong, the model learns that being sloppy 5% of the time is acceptable. There is no separate "ignore the bad ones" switch. Every example is a vote for how the model should behave.

The dinner party analogy

Imagine you are throwing a dinner party and you want great conversation. You could invite 500 random strangers, or 30 thoughtful, interesting guests. The 30 will produce a far better evening. A noisy crowd of 500 drowns out the good voices.

Datasets work the same way. A famous 2023 result ("LIMA") showed that ~1,000 carefully chosen examples could beat tens of thousands of mediocre ones. As of 2026, the best SFT Datasets teams still obsess over the guest list, not the headcount.

What curation actually involves

Curation is a set of decisions, made on purpose:

  • Relevance: Does this example match the job you are training for? A coding assistant does not need poetry.
  • Quality: Is the answer correct, well-written, and the kind of response you would be proud to ship?
  • Diversity: Do your examples cover the real range of things users will ask? Or are 800 of your 1,000 examples just "summarise this email"?
  • Difficulty mix: A few easy, many medium, some genuinely hard. All-easy teaches nothing; all-hard confuses.
  • Deduplication: Removing near-identical copies (more on this below).
  • Balance: No single style, topic, or answer length should dominate, unless you want the model to over-do it.

The salt analogy for deduplication

Duplicates are near-identical examples. A little repetition is like salt: a pinch helps the model remember. Too much and the dish is ruined.

If the same Q&A appears 200 times, the model memorises it word-for-word (this is overfitting: learning the training examples so exactly that it fails on anything new), and the rest of your data gets drowned out. Deduplication finds these copies. Exact duplicates are easy to catch; near-duplicates (same meaning, reworded) need Embeddings to spot, because two sentences can mean the same thing with zero shared words.

# Drop exact duplicates, then near-duplicates by meaning
import pandas as pd
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util

df = pd.read_json("raw.jsonl", lines=True)
df = df.drop_duplicates(subset=["prompt", "response"])  # exact

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
vecs = model.encode(df["prompt"].tolist())
sims = util.cos_sim(vecs, vecs)  # how similar is every pair?
# keep one of any pair scoring > 0.95 (manual step omitted for brevity)

A simple quality filter

You rarely eyeball a million rows by hand. A common 2026 pattern is to use a strong model as a judge (an "LLM-as-judge": one model scoring another's data) to rate each example, then keep only the top tier.

def keep(example):
    score = judge_rate(example["prompt"], example["response"])  # 1-5
    too_short = len(example["response"]) < 20
    return score >= 4 and not too_short

curated = [ex for ex in raw if keep(ex)]
print(f"Kept {len(curated)} of {len(raw)}")

Where this fits in real products

Say you are tuning a customer-support bot. You pull 100,000 past support chats. Curation is where you delete the angry rants, the off-topic chatter, the cases where the human agent gave wrong advice, and the 4,000 near-identical "how do I reset my password" tickets (keep maybe 30). You also make sure refunds, billing, and bug reports are all represented, not just password resets.

The 8,000 examples you keep will train a far better bot than the raw 100,000. This is the unglamorous work that separates a model that feels sharp from one that feels random. Curation also overlaps with Synthetic Datasets: when real examples are missing for some category, you generate new ones to fill the gap, then curate those too. Good curation is a loop, not a one-time pass: train, look at what the model still gets wrong, add and trim examples, repeat.

💡 Spend most of your fine-tuning effort here. A well-curated 5,000 examples almost always beats a lazy 200,000.
mentalModel: Curation is the dinner-party guest list, not the cleaning crew. You are not scrubbing each guest; you are deciding who gets an invite. A small room of thoughtful guests beats a stadium of randoms, because the model imitates everyone it meets.
takeaways: Curation picks which examples to keep; cleaning fixes broken ones.
Quality and diversity beat raw size — the model imitates every example.
Dedupe (especially near-dupes), balance categories, never leak test data.
mistakes: Chasing size over quality. Bragging "I have 1 million examples" while half are junk. The model imitates the junk too. A clean 5,000 usually wins.
Confusing curation with cleaning. Fixing formatting (cleaning, see Dataset Cleaning) does not decide whether a correct-but-irrelevant example belongs. Both jobs are needed.
Ignoring near-duplicates. You strip exact copies but miss 500 reworded versions of the same question, so the model overfits that one case and ignores everything else.
No diversity check. 80% of examples are one task type, so the model becomes great at that and useless at the rest. Always count examples per category.
Trusting an LLM judge blindly. Auto-scoring is fast but biased (it often loves long, confident answers). Spot-check by hand and calibrate the threshold.
Leaking test data into training. If your evaluation examples sneak into the training set, your scores look amazing and lie to you. Split first, curate second.
exercise:
Take any ~50 raw question-answer pairs you can find (export a chat log, scrape an FAQ, or hand-write them). Build a tiny curation pipeline in a notebook: (1) drop exact duplicate prompts, (2) tag each example with a category (e.g. billing, how-to, bug), (3) count examples per category and write one sentence on what is over- or under-represented, (4) keep only examples whose answer you would be happy to ship. Report how many of the 50 survived and why.

Stretch goal: Add near-duplicate detection. Embed each prompt with a sentence-transformer, compute pairwise cosine similarity, and remove one of every pair scoring above 0.92. Compare your final count to the exact-duplicate-only count.
solution: Hint: Load into a pandas DataFrame and use df.drop_duplicates(subset=["prompt"]) for step 1. For categories, a quick df.groupby("category").size() reveals the imbalance instantly. For the stretch goal, SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2").encode(...) plus util.cos_sim gives a similarity matrix; loop the upper triangle and mark the second item of any pair above your threshold for removal. Expect near-dedup to cut noticeably more than exact-dedup, which is the whole point.
summary: Data curation is choosing which examples to train on, separate from cleaning (fixing them). Quality and diversity beat raw quantity, because the model imitates every example it sees. Deduplicate (especially near-duplicates), balance categories, filter for correctness, and never leak test data. Treat it as an iterative loop, and spend most of your fine-tuning effort here.

keyTerms.def: The deliberate process of selecting which training examples to include and which to discard, based on relevance, quality, diversity and balance.
Curation decides which examples belong; cleaning fixes broken or badly formatted examples. Two different jobs, both required.
Removing copies of examples. Exact dedup catches identical text; near-dedup catches reworded versions with the same meaning.
Two examples that mean the same thing despite different wording, found using embeddings (numeric meaning vectors) and similarity scores.
When a model learns its training examples so exactly (often from too many duplicates) that it performs badly on new, unseen inputs.
How well your examples span the real range of tasks, topics and difficulty users will actually throw at the model.
Using a strong language model to automatically score the quality of training examples, so you can filter at scale instead of by hand.
When evaluation/test examples accidentally appear in the training set, making scores look great but meaningless.

diagram.svg: <svg class="dg" viewBox="0 0 720 340" role="img" aria-label="A raw dataset funneled through curation filters into a small high-quality set"><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="12" markerHeight="12" refX="9" refY="5" orient="auto"><path class="dg-arrowhead" d="M0,0 L11,5 L0,10 z"/></marker></defs><text class="dg-title" x="360" y="34" text-anchor="middle">Curation: many in, few good out</text><rect class="dg-box dg-box-warn" x="30" y="130" width="150" height="80" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="105" y="165" text-anchor="middle">Raw data</text><text class="dg-sub" x="105" y="186" text-anchor="middle">100,000 messy</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="180" y1="170" x2="250" y2="170" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box dg-box-accent" x="250" y="70" width="190" height="200" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="345" y="100" text-anchor="middle">Curation filters</text><text class="dg-sub" x="345" y="130" text-anchor="middle">Relevance + quality</text><text class="dg-sub" x="345" y="154" text-anchor="middle">Diversity + balance</text><text class="dg-sub" x="345" y="178" text-anchor="middle">Dedupe near-copies</text><text class="dg-sub" x="345" y="202" text-anchor="middle">No test-data leak</text><text class="dg-note" x="345" y="238" text-anchor="middle">keep only the best</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="440" y1="170" x2="510" y2="170" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box dg-box-good" x="510" y="130" width="180" height="80" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="600" y="165" text-anchor="middle">Curated set</text><text class="dg-sub" x="600" y="186" text-anchor="middle">~8,000 great</text><path class="dg-edge" d="M600,210 C600,290 345,300 345,272" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><text class="dg-note" x="472" y="320" text-anchor="middle">iterate: train, find gaps, refine</text></svg>
diagram.caption: A huge messy dataset passes through curation filters to become a small, high-quality set — then the loop repeats.

quiz.q: Your teammate proudly says the fine-tuning set has 1,000,000 examples, but roughly 30% are wrong or off-topic. What is the main risk?
options: The model will simply skip the bad examples during training
The model learns to imitate the bad behavior about 30% of the time
Training will refuse to start until the data is perfect
Larger datasets always produce better models, so there is no risk
quiz.explain: There is no 'ignore the junk' switch — the model imitates every example, so bad data teaches bad behavior. A smaller, well-curated set usually wins.

quiz.q: What is the key difference between data curation and data cleaning?
options: Cleaning chooses which examples to keep; curation fixes formatting
They are two names for the exact same task
Curation chooses which examples belong; cleaning fixes broken or messy ones
Curation only applies to images, cleaning only to text
quiz.explain: Curation is the guest-list decision (who is in or out); cleaning scrubs the formatting of the examples you keep. Both jobs are needed but they are distinct.

quiz.q: You removed all exact-duplicate prompts, yet the model still memorizes one common question. What most likely happened?
options: Hundreds of near-duplicates (same meaning, reworded) survived
You should have kept the exact duplicates instead
Cleaning the formatting would have fixed the memorization
The model is too small to memorize anything
quiz.explain: Exact dedup misses reworded copies that mean the same thing. Catching those needs embeddings and similarity scoring; too many copies cause overfitting.

quiz.q: You filter examples with an LLM-as-judge and keep only its top-rated ones. What is the smartest safeguard?
options: Trust the judge fully — automated scores are unbiased
Only keep the very longest answers, since they score highest
Spot-check kept examples by hand and calibrate the score threshold
Skip the judge and keep every example to be safe
quiz.explain: LLM judges are fast but biased — they often favor long, confident answers. Hand spot-checking and tuning the threshold keeps the auto-filter honest.
